Skeletal muscle has intracellular buffers which attempt to buffer the acidity caused by decreases in pH resulting from lactic acid and other acids during HIE. One such (major) buffer is carnosine. Recent evidence has shown that ingesting 4 g/day of β-alanine over a period of 4 weeks or more increases the muscle carnosine content. Figure 9.18 exhibits how β-alanine promotes muscle carnosine and thereby improves high intensity performance (Castell *et al*., 2010; MacLaren, 2011).

Figure 9.19 portrays the likely sports in which β-alanine supplementation may have a positive impact. A noteworthy treatise on the impact and efficacy of β-alanine supplementation is that of Saunders *et al*. (2017).

## Definition

β-alanine is a dietary supplement precursor amino acid that, when ingested chronically, elevates skeletal muscle carnosine concentration, thereby enhancing intracellular buffering capacity against exercise-induced acidosis.

## Core Principles

- During high-intensity exercise (HIE), accumulation of lactic acid and other acids lowers intramuscular pH, which is opposed by intracellular buffers.
- Carnosine is a major intracellular buffer in skeletal muscle.
- Oral β-alanine supplementation increases muscle carnosine content, augmenting the buffering reservoir.
- Effective dosing: ≥4 g/day of β-alanine for ≥4 weeks raises muscle carnosine, which in turn improves high-intensity exercise performance.

## Sub-concepts

- Intracellular buffering systems in skeletal muscle
- Carnosine as a histidine-containing dipeptide buffer
- β-alanine as the rate-limiting precursor for carnosine synthesis
- Loading kinetics: chronic dosing (weeks) required for meaningful muscle carnosine elevation

_(尚無內容)_

## Practical Applications

- Supplementation protocol: 4 g/day of β-alanine for 4 weeks or longer to raise muscle carnosine.
- Likely beneficial sports: those reliant on high-intensity efforts where acidosis limits performance (per Figure 9.19).
- Saunders *et al.* (2017) provides a noteworthy treatise on the impact and efficacy of β-alanine supplementation for practitioners evaluating its use.

## Related Concepts

- Carnosine
- Intracellular buffering / muscle pH regulation
- Lactic acid and exercise-induced acidosis
- High-intensity exercise (HIE) performance
